BUSINESS GLOSSARY — E-COMMERCE DOMAIN
==========================================

This document defines the core business concepts and their relationships in the e-commerce platform. These concepts represent the shared understanding between business stakeholders and technical teams.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CUSTOMER
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Definition: A Customer is any individual or legal entity that has registered an account on the platform and has made at least one purchase.

Key Attributes:
- Customers are identified by a unique Customer ID
- Customers must provide a valid email address during registration
- Customers may have one or more associated delivery addresses
- Each customer has a geographic region code for tax purposes
- Customers can be marked as active or inactive

Business Rules:
- A customer must place at least one order to remain in the active database
- A customer may have multiple addresses but only one primary address
- Customer data is retained for 7 years after last purchase for compliance

Synonyms: client, buyer, account holder, user, registered user
Related Concepts: CustomerAddress, SalesOrder

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PRODUCT
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Definition: A Product is a tangible or digital item offered for sale on the platform.

Key Attributes:
- Each Product is uniquely identified by a SKU (Stock Keeping Unit) code
- Products have a human-readable product name
- Products have a defined unit price in the company's base currency
- Products have an active/inactive status flag
- Each product belongs to exactly one Category
- Products maintain inventory levels across multiple warehouse locations

Business Rules:
- A product must belong to a valid category before it can be sold
- Product prices can change daily based on market conditions
- Inactive products cannot be ordered but remain in the catalog for historical orders
- Digital products have infinite inventory

Synonyms: item, article, goods, merchandise, SKU
Related Concepts: Category, Inventory, OrderLineItem

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
SALES ORDER
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Definition: A Sales Order is a formal transactional document that records the agreement between the platform and a Customer to supply one or more Products at specified quantities and prices, on a specific date.

Key Attributes:
- Each Sales Order has a unique Order ID
- Orders reference exactly one Customer who placed the order
- Orders have an order date when the transaction was initiated
- Orders have a total monetary value calculated as the sum of all line items
- Orders have a status: PENDING, CONFIRMED, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, or CANCELLED
- Orders must contain at least one Order Line Item

Business Rules:
- An order cannot be shipped until payment is confirmed
- An order can only be cancelled if it has not yet been shipped
- Order status changes are logged for audit purposes
- Orders under €100 require manager approval for certain payment methods

Synonyms: order, purchase order, transaction, customer order, sales transaction
Related Concepts: Customer, OrderLineItem, Invoice

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ORDER LINE ITEM
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Definition: An Order Line Item is a single line within a Sales Order that specifies a particular Product, the quantity ordered, and the agreed unit price at time of purchase.

Key Attributes:
- Each line item has a unique line identifier
- Line items reference exactly one Product
- Line items specify the quantity of units ordered
- Line items record the unit price at the time of order (not current price)
- The extended amount equals quantity multiplied by unit price

Business Rules:
- A Sales Order must contain at least one line item
- Line items cannot be modified after order confirmation
- The unit price on the line item is fixed and does not change with future price updates
- Line items for discontinued products show the product name at time of order

Synonyms: line item, order detail, order row, sales line, order line
Related Concepts: SalesOrder, Product, InvoiceLine

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CATEGORY
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Definition: A Category is a hierarchical classification grouping related Products.

Key Attributes:
- Each Category has a unique identifier
- Categories have human-readable names
- Categories can have parent Categories, forming a tree structure
- Each Product belongs to exactly one leaf Category
- Categories can be marked as visible or hidden in the storefront

Business Rules:
- The category tree has a maximum depth of 5 levels
- Root categories represent major product divisions (e.g., Electronics, Clothing)
- A category cannot be deleted if it contains products
- Category changes affect product navigation and search filters

Example Category Hierarchy:
- Electronics (root)
  - Computers
    - Laptops
    - Desktops
  - Phones
    - Smartphones
    - Accessories

Synonyms: product category, product type, product group, classification
Related Concepts: Product

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CUSTOMER ADDRESS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Definition: A Customer Address is a postal or geographic address associated with a Customer, used for delivery or billing purposes.

Key Attributes:
- Each address has a unique identifier
- Addresses are associated with exactly one Customer
- Addresses have a type: DELIVERY or BILLING
- Addresses contain street, city, postal code, and country information
- One address per customer can be marked as primary

Business Rules:
- A customer may have multiple addresses
- Delivery and billing addresses can be different
- International orders require valid country codes
- Postal codes are validated against country-specific formats

Synonyms: delivery address, shipping address, billing address, postal address
Related Concepts: Customer, SalesOrder

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INVENTORY
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Definition: Inventory refers to the quantity of a Product currently available in a specific warehouse location.

Key Attributes:
- Each inventory record references one Product
- Each inventory record references one Warehouse location
- Inventory records track the quantity on hand
- Inventory levels are timestamped with last update time

Business Rules:
- Inventory levels are updated in real-time with each Sales Order confirmation
- Inventory levels increase when products are restocked
- Low inventory triggers automatic reorder notifications
- Products with zero inventory cannot be ordered
- Warehouse transfers update inventory at both locations

Business Importance:
- Accurate inventory prevents overselling
- Inventory data drives purchasing decisions
- Inventory levels affect product availability on the website

Synonyms: stock, available quantity, stock level, on-hand quantity
Related Concepts: Product, Warehouse, SalesOrder

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
WAREHOUSE
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Definition: A Warehouse is a physical storage location where Products are stored and from which they are shipped to Customers.

Key Attributes:
- Each warehouse has a unique code
- Warehouses have a physical address
- Warehouses are located in specific geographic regions
- Warehouses have storage capacity limits

Business Rules:
- Each warehouse maintains inventory for multiple products
- Orders are fulfilled from the warehouse closest to the customer
- Warehouse transfers are used to balance inventory across locations
- Warehouse capacity is monitored to prevent overstocking

Synonyms: distribution center, fulfillment center, storage location, depot
Related Concepts: Inventory, Product, CustomerAddress

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
KEY RELATIONSHIP SUMMARY
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Customer Relationships:
- A Customer places zero or more Sales Orders
- A Customer has one or more Customer Addresses
- A Customer is associated with exactly one geographic Region

Product Relationships:
- A Product belongs to exactly one Category
- A Product has Inventory records in one or more Warehouses
- A Product appears on zero or more Order Line Items

Sales Order Relationships:
- A Sales Order is placed by exactly one Customer
- A Sales Order contains one or more Order Line Items
- A Sales Order is delivered to exactly one Customer Address

Order Line Item Relationships:
- An Order Line Item is part of exactly one Sales Order
- An Order Line Item references exactly one Product
- An Order Line Item has a quantity and unit price

Category Relationships:
- A Category contains zero or more Products
- A Category may have one parent Category
- A Category may have zero or more child Categories

Inventory Relationships:
- An Inventory record tracks one Product in one Warehouse
- A Product has Inventory records in one or more Warehouses
- A Warehouse has Inventory records for zero or more Products

Address Relationships:
- A Customer Address belongs to exactly one Customer
- A Customer has one or more Customer Addresses

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
BUSINESS PROCESSES
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Order Fulfillment Process:
1. Customer places an order through the web storefront
2. System validates product availability in inventory
3. Payment is processed and confirmed
4. Order status changes from PENDING to CONFIRMED
5. Warehouse receives picking instructions
6. Products are picked and packed
7. Order status changes to SHIPPED
8. Customer receives delivery confirmation
9. Order status changes to DELIVERED

Inventory Management Process:
1. Products are received from suppliers at warehouses
2. Inventory records are updated to reflect new stock
3. When orders are placed, inventory is reserved
4. Upon order confirmation, inventory is decremented
5. Low inventory triggers automatic reorder
6. Warehouse transfers balance inventory across locations

Customer Management Process:
1. New customers register through the website
2. Customer provides email and creates password
3. Customer may add delivery and billing addresses
4. Customer places first order to become active
5. Customer activity is tracked for marketing purposes
6. Inactive customers are identified for re-engagement campaigns
